title: Alpes Bank - When Tradition Meets Artificial Intelligence
tagline: A long-established Swiss universal bank must decide how to use generative AI without putting its core business at risk.
review_notes: English version of the manual BWL A teaching case. All names fictional.

sections.title: Alpes Bank: A Company Under Pressure
sections.content:
Alpes Bank is one of Switzerland's largest universal banks. For more than one hundred years, it has stood for personal advice, reliability, and direct contact with customers in its branches. The company employs 4,000 people and serves around one million customers across Switzerland.

While many competitors have invested heavily in digital channels in recent years, Alpes Bank has deliberately held on to its branch network. Its customers are 51 years old on average, have high incomes, and place great value on personal support. They are willing to pay higher prices for good service. The branch remains the most profitable business area - far ahead of online banking or the app (see Exhibit 1).

But this proven model suddenly comes under pressure: competing banks are beginning to offer AI-supported advisory tools at a fraction of the cost of a human advisor. The board reacts with a question no one had dared to ask out loud: 'If AI can provide financial advice for a few cents, how long can we still justify our expensive branch model?'

sections.title: The Mandate: Prove Generative AI
sections.content:
As a defensive measure, the board decides to launch an internal initiative. Tamara Maurer, Head of Digital Transformation at Alpes Bank, receives a clear mandate from the CEO: 'By the end of the quarter, I want to see a GenAI pilot - something that can be rolled out quickly, shows clear business value, and does not break anything.'

Tamara has three months. She may bypass some internal processes, but only as long as she demonstrates clear added value. Internally, the organization is skeptical: Alpes Bank has a traditional, risk-averse culture. Anyone who returns with a failure is unlikely to get a second chance.

Tamara brings together a small team of data scientists, IT specialists, and business analysts. In a one-day workshop, they jointly develop three ideas for a first GenAI use case (see Exhibit 2). By the end of the day, the team must agree on a single use case.

sections.title: Implementation: Between Speed and Control
sections.content:
The team chooses the email assistant. Over twelve weeks, across four sprints, Alpes Bank develops a working system together with the external startup AILabs. The process is simple: a customer sends an email, the assistant analyzes the request, searches the internal knowledge base, and suggests a complete draft response to the customer service employee - in German, French, Italian, and English, within ten seconds.

The path is not smooth. In sprint 3, a technical problem appears that the team thought it had already solved: the system often fails to understand customer messages correctly because customers phrase their requests in many different ways. Parts of the system have to be revised, even though they had already been marked as 'done'. This frustrates the bank team, which is used to completing projects in clearly separated phases.

In sprint 4, another problem becomes visible: customers from Graubunden often write in Standard German but include individual Romansh words. The assistant often fails to interpret this mixture correctly, so customers from this region receive weaker answers than others. AILabs recommends delaying the rollout until the issue is fixed. Tamara sees it differently and decides on a limited rollout labelled as 'experimental', with a human employee as the final control point.

The result: the email assistant reduces the average processing time for simple requests by 21 percent. The system works, but it is not perfect.

sections.title: Governance: Who Is Responsible?
sections.content:
After the successful pilot, the CEO sets a clear condition: without a GenAI-specific governance model, the system will not be rolled out further. Tamara presents her concept to the board with three guiding principles - competent, compliant, and calculated (the 3C model):

Competent: Customer service employees must be able to assess, correct, and report errors in AI-generated responses. They are the first control point.

Compliant: Every change to the system is documented and reviewed. Data protection and traceability are non-negotiable.

Calculated: Benefits are measured continuously - time saved, error rates, customer satisfaction. If the indicators develop negatively, the rollout is stopped.

The CEO is convinced, but warns: 'The real effort starts now. The business units are not used to taking responsibility for technology. We first have to build that capability.'

sections.title: One Year Later: Growth and Its Limits
sections.content:
One year after the pilot, Alpes Bank has several GenAI applications in use: the email assistant, an internal knowledge copilot, and an HR chatbot for routine questions. Employees appreciate the support. The numbers look good.

But the CEO is dissatisfied: 'We are using GenAI to make the old structures a little more efficient. But we are not really thinking in a new way yet.' He sees the greatest potential not in automating individual tasks, but in redesigning entire business processes across departmental boundaries.

Tamara faces a strategic question: how must the organization be rebuilt so that GenAI does not only work within existing silos, but creates real value along the entire value chain? The way she answers this question will help determine the future of Alpes Bank.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 1: Average profit per customer by channel (in CHF)
exhibits.content:
Channel             | 2015  | 2020  | 2025  | Industry average 2025
Branch              | 120   | 10    | 140   | 80
Email               | 20    | 80    | 30    | 20
Phone               | 70    | 100   | 80    | 20
Online banking      | 0     | 20    | 10    | 60
Website             | 10    | 40    | 30    | 40
App                 | 0     | 10    | 0     | 60
Total               | 220   | 260   | 290   | 290

Note: Negative values in individual years reflect high initial investments. The jump in branch profit from 2020 to 2025 is explained by targeted premiumization of the advisory offering.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 2: Rough assessment of the three GenAI use cases
exhibits.content:
Metric                              | Email assistant        | Internal knowledge copilot | Website chatbot
Annual interactions                 | 300,000 emails         | 200,000 requests          | 100,000 help requests
Time saved (avg. per case)          | 2 minutes              | 5 minutes                 | 2 minutes
Annual hours saved                  | 10,000 h               | 16,667 h                  | 3,333 h
Cost per hour                       | CHF 35 (agent)         | CHF 50 (knowledge worker)  | CHF 35 (agent)
Annual cost savings (from Y1)       | CHF 350,000            | CHF 833,000                | CHF 117,000
Initial costs (Y0)                  | CHF 160,000            | CHF 800,000                | CHF 110,000
Ongoing operating costs (from Y1)   | CHF 80,000             | CHF 300,000                | CHF 45,000
Annual gross profit (from Y1)       | CHF 270,000            | CHF 533,000                | CHF 72,000
Estimated time to MVP               | approx. 12 weeks       | approx. 27 weeks           | approx. 10 weeks

Note: These figures are rough estimates from the ideation workshop. They do not include investments in employee development or possible resistance during implementation.

exhibits.title: Exhibit 3: Management voices on the governance question
exhibits.content:
CEO: 'We have shown that GenAI can create real business value. The question now is whether we can control the risk in order to continue. Without governance, we will not roll this out further.'

Tamara Maurer (Head of Digital Transformation): 'GenAI will always hallucinate a little. The real governance question is not: how do we eliminate hallucinations? It is: where are we willing to carry this risk, and are we able to manage it?'

Head of Customer Service: 'My employees are good at communicating with customers. But now they are suddenly supposed to assess AI outputs as well? That is a new capability. It does not appear on its own.'

AILabs technical lead Lucien Getaz: 'From a technical perspective, the system is ready. But ready from a technical perspective and ready from a bank perspective are two very different things. We first had to learn that in this project.'

questions.text: Identify the two challenges that are most critical for the future development of Alpes Bank. For each challenge, explain what it consists of concretely and why it is relevant for the business model. For at least one challenge, show why it results from the interaction between external developments and internal conditions.
evaluation_focus: Identifiziert die kritischsten Herausforderungen der Bank statt nur Symptome aufzuzählen.
Verknüpft jede Herausforderung sichtbar mit dem Geschäftsmodell von Alpes Bank.
Zeigt mindestens bei einer Herausforderung das Zusammenspiel von externem Druck und internen Gegebenheiten.

required_canvas_blocks.label: Customer Relationships
required_canvas_blocks.expectation: Erkennt, wie KI-Druck und Serviceversprechen die Kundenbeziehung und das Premium-Beratungsmodell tangieren.

required_canvas_blocks.label: Channels
required_canvas_blocks.expectation: Zeigt, dass die Herausforderung mit der Kanalstruktur und dem Spannungsfeld Filiale versus digitale Kanäle zusammenhängt.

required_canvas_blocks.label: Revenue Streams
required_canvas_blocks.expectation: Macht deutlich, warum die Herausforderung für Erträge, Zahlungsbereitschaft oder Profitabilität des Modells relevant ist.

calibration_notes: Die Aufgabe verlangt zwei klar getrennte Herausforderungen; nur eine Herausforderung begrenzt den Score deutlich.
Externer Druck und interne Gegebenheiten müssen bei mindestens einer Herausforderung als Wechselwirkung sichtbar sein.
Lehrende bestrafen reine Piloten-/Umsetzungsprobleme, wenn die strategische Geschäftsmodellfrage verfehlt wird.

questions.text: Tamara must decide which GenAI use case to prioritize. Develop a clear recommendation: which of the three use cases should Alpes Bank implement first, and what should the long-term competitive advantage be based on? Name concretely which internal strengths support this advantage, why it is difficult for competitors to imitate, and which central trade-off your recommendation creates.
allowed_frameworks: Resource-based logic (VRIO principle, without naming it)
evaluation_focus: Empfiehlt einen der drei Use Cases klar und begründet die Wahl mit langfristiger Wettbewerbslogik.
Zeigt, welche internen Stärken den Vorteil tragen und warum sie schwer imitierbar sind.
Benannt einen echten Zielkonflikt statt nur Chancen aufzuzählen.

required_canvas_blocks.label: Value Propositions
required_canvas_blocks.expectation: Leitet her, welchen konkreten Kundennutzen der priorisierte Use Case schafft und wie er das Leistungsversprechen verändert.

required_canvas_blocks.label: Key Resources
required_canvas_blocks.expectation: Verbindet den empfohlenen Vorteil mit tragenden internen Ressourcen oder Fähigkeiten von Alpes Bank.

required_canvas_blocks.label: Cost Structure
required_canvas_blocks.expectation: Adressiert den Zielkonflikt zwischen Nutzen, Aufwand, Risiko oder Effizienz des gewählten Use Cases.

calibration_notes: Ohne eindeutige Priorisierung eines der drei definierten Use Cases bleibt der Score niedrig.
Langfristiger Wettbewerbsvorteil braucht interne Stärken und Imitationsschutz; bloße Effizienzbehauptungen reichen nicht.
Ein Zielkonflikt muss aus der eigenen Empfehlung folgen, nicht nur allgemein als Risiko erwähnt werden.

questions.text: Tamara decides to develop the AI solution with the external partner AILabs instead of building everything internally. Make a reasoned make-or-buy decision for the development of the email assistant: which factors - such as how specific the required capability is, how often it is needed, and how uncertain the quality requirements are - speak for or against an external solution? What concrete risk arises from your decision, and what measure would you take?
evaluation_focus: Trifft eine klare Make-or-Buy-Entscheidung für den E-Mail-Assistenten.
Diskutiert Spezifität, Häufigkeit und Unsicherheit nachvollziehbar.
Leitet ein konkretes Risiko und eine passende Gegenmassnahme ab.

required_canvas_blocks.label: Key Partners
required_canvas_blocks.expectation: Bewertet die Rolle des externen Partners explizit als Teil der Wertschöpfungslogik und Governance der Bank.

required_canvas_blocks.label: Key Activities
required_canvas_blocks.expectation: Zeigt, welche kritischen Aktivitäten intern bleiben sollten und welche extern erbracht werden können.

required_canvas_blocks.label: Key Resources
required_canvas_blocks.expectation: Begründet die Entscheidung mit Blick auf schwer ersetzbare interne Ressourcen oder Wissen der Bank.

calibration_notes: Die Entscheidung muss Make-or-Buy-Faktoren nutzen: Spezifität, Häufigkeit und Unsicherheit.
Eine klare Entscheidung plus Risiko reicht nur teilweise, wenn Key Partners, Key Activities und Key Resources implizit bleiben.
Lehrende bewerten fehlende Canvas-Verankerung strenger als eine nur plausible allgemeine Outsourcing-Argumentation.

questions.text: Consider your decisions from the previous tasks as a whole. Which strategic direction should Alpes Bank prioritize over the next 12-24 months, and which of your decisions do you consider the riskiest in retrospect? Explain why this decision carries the greatest risk and show what consequences revising this decision would have for the other parts of your recommendation.
allowed_frameworks: Business model logic (value proposition, value creation, revenue)
evaluation_focus: Integriert die früheren Entscheidungen zu einer konsistenten strategischen Stossrichtung.
Identifiziert die riskanteste Entscheidung und erklärt die Interdependenzen bei einer Revision.
Denkt in Wertschöpfung über Silos hinweg statt isoliert nach Funktionen.

required_canvas_blocks.label: Value Propositions
required_canvas_blocks.expectation: Verankert die strategische Stossrichtung sichtbar im zukünftigen Leistungsversprechen der Bank.

required_canvas_blocks.label: Key Activities
required_canvas_blocks.expectation: Zeigt, welche Aktivitäten oder Prozessketten priorisiert umgebaut werden müssen.

required_canvas_blocks.label: Key Resources
required_canvas_blocks.expectation: Benennt die kritischen Fähigkeiten oder Ressourcen, die für die strategische Richtung aufgebaut oder geschützt werden müssen.

required_canvas_blocks.label: Cost Structure
required_canvas_blocks.expectation: Macht die Kosten-, Risiko- oder Governance-Folgen der priorisierten Richtung transparent.

calibration_notes: Die Antwort muss frühere Entscheidungen integriert betrachten; eine isolierte neue Strategie ist nur teilweise passend.
Die riskanteste Entscheidung soll vergleichend begründet werden, nicht nur genannt.
Eine Revision muss Konsequenzen für andere Empfehlungsteile zeigen; bloße Wiederholung der Präferenz reicht nicht.
